speaker: interviewer
text: This is Mary Beth Fiske. And I'm interviewing Anneliese Schwab. And this is tape number 5.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very important thing that I'd like to point out is that the family in Germany who were responsible for literally saving our lives and seeing to it that we got out of the country [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] live on. That is, there is one remaining sister. Although in 1970, all four sisters were still alive. And from the time that packages were allowed to be sent after the war from the United States to Germany, my parents, of course, picked up from there. And since there was a great deal of hunger and need at the time after the end of the war, oh, we sent packages of food. So right, right from 1945 or '46 on, we remained in touch with these-- with this family and send them packages of food to help them get back on their feet [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 19-- [SIGHS] I believe it was '70-- I wanted to, first of all, to see this family again and also see what my memories were all about. And we were meeting my Israeli cousin, the oldest-- the second oldest from the cousins, actually, the one who ended up in Israel, and his wife in Europe. We have maintained very close contact with the few remaining members of the family, specifically, the two cousins that I grew up with. And so they met us. And I was, at first, very reluctant to go into Germany. But with their assistance to somehow get us over the hurdle, Paul took me back to the family places and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] spent more than two days showing me all the important places that, of course, I really couldn't have located on my own. The memories are there. I remember my uncle Karl taking me to the cemetery to my grandfather's grave before we left, which was a very emotional experience. And at the time, he said to me, never forget this is where your grandfather is buried. But I needed Paul-- I needed Paul to walk me through it all. And I had a thousand questions. And so they met us. And Paul took me on this tour of ancient history, [LAUGHS] as we called it, family history. And, and that sort of eased the way for me from which point on our youngest son, Tom, was with us. We went to visit this family in Aachen, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very emotional reunion. In the meantime, I had-- I was at the age that my mother was when, when we left Germany. And our son was the age-- a little younger than I was-- when we actually left. And so when we met Christine Bianbaer, we just sat, and we cried. We cried all the time. She kept touching me. And It was just one of those very emotional reunions. And since then, we have gone back two more times to visit. Christine has since died. The other two sisters have died. But there's one remaining sister who is alive today. And she's having her 88th birthday shortly. And we go back. And we are in touch with her on a constant basis. And so when our oldest granddaughter, Rachel, was bat mitzvahed, her father had the idea that he would like to take her to Auschwitz for her Jewish education. So I was really dead set against it. And so I talked to him and didn't have to talk to my daughter very much. Brenda was very much in tune with me, definitely is-- that we would-- we would go to Europe and meet them. And I would-- we would house them through 10 days or a little more than that in Germany. And I would take them all to all the important places that I felt and also introduce her to this remaining sister in Aachen, whose name is Herta Bianbaer. And so we had a most meaningful experience as I showed them places where we used to have family picnics right outside of the-- of the town. The town has been totally rebuilt and looks very different. Astonishingly enough, we found when we went back the first time that our house had survived the severe bombing. It was an industrial town. And it was severely bombed by the Americans. Our house had remained standing on that block because the hospital was across the street. And it had a very large Red Cross painted on the roof and somehow was spared. And through introduction through someone, I actually brought up the courage to go to the house and say hello to the young couple who are living there now and introducing myself. They had absolutely no knowledge of what had transpired. [LAUGHS] I had to bring them up to date--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the Nazi era.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I entered, the woman said, should I show you around the house? And I said, well, no. Before you do that, let me tell you, the living room is here. The dining room is there. The bedrooms are there and there. And she was absolutely dumbfounded that I knew every corner of the house from memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was particularly meaningful having a daughter and our granddaughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and our son-in-law walk through the house where I grew up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the street where I grew up and had some of my experiences. And of course, we went to visit the graves of my uncle Karl and also my grandfather and the place where the synagogue had been, where it was burned to the ground. And memorable spots-- the house where my grandmother and my uncle and aunt lived, of course, are rebuilt in a modern style. But the location of it and how I would go to school. And, you know, it's sort of very personal things. And then, of course, we went to visit with Herta, who baked two cakes and who lives still in the old-world style. She stores all her preserves in the cellar-- in the cool cellar with her potatoes. And she has no hot running water in the house. And it, it, it it was both emotional and instructive and just a memory never to forget, really. I hope to be able to do it for the other grandchildren. [LAUGHS] You need a lot of-- a lot of footwork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I hope I can still introduce them to some of this. And thankfully, through this tape, they will be able to understand what brought us here and why so many times [LAUGHS] I weep when emotional things come up. It's out of gratitude, gratitude for all the wonderful things that we've been able to enjoy, [CRYING] which I can't emphasize enough. Because it's been a blessed life. And I'm deeply grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you. This is a picture of what Duren looked like when I was growing up. And the house where my uncle had his office and my grandmother and my aunt and uncle lived would be on, on the idea of what you see on the left of the picture. But this was a typical view of, of a town street. This is a picture of my grandmother, my paternal grandmother. Her name is Auguste Marx. And that would have been taken and somewheres around perhaps 1935 or 1936 on-- in the backyard of her residence where she lived in Duren. This is a picture of my uncle Karl, Karl Marx, and also-- a picture taken around 1936 before we left Germany. This is a picture of the synagogue in Duren where we attended as a family-- religious services. And on the bottom is a picture of the cantor at the bimah in the synagogue. Adjoining to that was our schoolhouse. The picture was taken approximately, again, around 1936. This is a picture of my youngest cousin that I grew up with. His name was Karl Heinz Marx. He was 16 years old when that picture was taken in Duren. And he perished in Poland at one of the extermination camps. This is a picture of Herbert Kramer taken in 1939. He is the business associate and dearest friend who spirited us out of the country in his car to Belgium. On the right is Christine Bianbaer, his accomplice and my mother's dear friend. The picture also taken in 1939. And they were responsible for our getting out of the country and making it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where or when these photographs were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about 1939. Did I mention that?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's passport in Belgium. And as was mandatory at the time, the very large J for Jewish is stamped on it. And it, it gives her the Jewish name that she had to adopt in Germany, which was Sara. So it's Anna Sara Marx. And this was taken in 1949 at the-- at the consulate.

speaker: interviewer
text: It says 1940 on this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's somewhat confusing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not '49.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not '39? It was '40?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think it was in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Leon Spetter and his wife, Fanny Spetter, S-P-E-T-T-E-R. And this was in Paris, our second guardian angel who managed to supply my father with money in order to get us out of France.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date of the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date of the photo would be 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was taken in 1971 when we went back to Germany for the first time to meet with Christine and her family, her other sisters. Christine is the fourth one from the left. And she was suffering from, from an illness then. And she is somewhat bowed over. And that was a very emotional reunion because it's the first time that we saw her since she had helped us out of the country. This picture was taken in 1948, the 4th of July, when Henry and I were married. And behind us is Henry's mother and his father, Ruth and Martin, and my parents, Rudolf Marx and Anna Marx.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, in the middle, is-- are Henry and I, starting our journey together in 1948 and still going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of the marker that is placed where the synagogue was burned down in Duren. This picture was taken in 1994 when we went to Europe to revisit the town-- our son-in-law Paul, our daughter Brenda, our granddaughter Rachel, and myself. This is a picture taken in 1994 when we visited the cemetery. It's the monument where my grandfather is buried-- actually buried there. And the other names on the monument are engraved in memory of the family members that were lost in the camp-- my aunt Clementine Bender; her husband Bertold Bender; Hugo Marx and Bianca Marx, who were also lost in Holland; and Karl Heinz, who was lost in Poland; and also my grandmother, Auguste Marx, who was lost in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at Buchenwald in 1945. On the right hand side, the soldier is Henry Schwab, my husband now. And that is at the liberation of the camp when General Patton was there. In the center is one of the inmates of the camp that had been liberated. That didn't go so smooth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our daughter Brenda. The picture is taken in front of the gate at Buchenwald. And it shows 50 years later that these camps are being visited by members of families who had been there. This is a picture of my maternal grandfather and grandmother, Herbert Stern and Pauline Stern. And this picture would have been taken in the late 1930s of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Germany in the town where they lived, which was Bielefeld-Westfalen This, this picture was taken in front of my former home in Duren in 1994 when our daughter and her husband and our granddaughter went to visit with the family places in Duren. When I was growing up, that house was not as colorful. It was just gray stucco. This is part of our wonderful family-- my husband Henry on my left; our son John next to me; our son Thomas on the right of him; our daughter Brenda; and our granddaughter Lauren seated in front of us. Unfortunately, some of them weren't able to be here. Our granddaughter Rachael and our grandson Aaron are up in Vermont going to school. And of course the in-law-- and, of course, the in-laws-- the daughter-in-law Stacy Schwab, and our son-in-law Paul Gerber. My heart's--
